items.correct: When the audience knows something a character does not, creating tension between the character's words/actions and the audience's understanding.
items.misconception: Dramatic irony is when something ironic happens in a play.
items.random_wrong: Dramatic irony is when characters speak in dramatic voices.
items.why: Students treat 'dramatic irony' as just irony in a drama. The defining feature is the gap between audience and character knowledge — without that gap, it isn't dramatic irony.

items.correct: When a speaker says one thing but means another — typically the opposite — relying on shared context for the listener to detect the gap.
items.misconception: Verbal irony is just saying something unexpected.
items.random_wrong: Verbal irony is when you make a joke about something.
items.why: Students conflate irony with surprise or humour. Irony specifically involves a gap between literal and intended meaning — a more precise device than 'unexpected'.

items.q: What is the difference between a theme and a symbol?
items.correct: A theme is an abstract idea or concern the text explores (e.g. mortality); a symbol is a concrete image that stands for something else (e.g. a skull).
items.misconception: Themes are just symbols repeated through the book.
items.random_wrong: A theme is the title of the book.
items.why: Students treat theme and symbol as the same thing. Themes are ideas, symbols are images. Skulls (symbol) can develop the theme (mortality), but they are not the same level of abstraction.

items.q: Who delivers the Queen Mab speech?
items.why: It's Mercutio. “Romeo” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What does Juliet compare Romeo to in the balcony scene?
items.why: It's The sun. “A rose” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In which Act is the balcony scene?
items.why: The real figure is 2. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
